commentary: We just open-sourced the ridgeline-queue Go library — our internal distributed task queue, battle-tested across three years of production workloads. Zero external dependencies, exactly-once delivery semantics. Link in comments.

We are hiring two senior backend engineers for the Ridgeline Cloud platform team. Remote-friendly across US time zones. If you enjoy working close to the metal on distributed Go systems — DM me or check our careers page.

ridgeline-queue v1.8.0 is live. Consumer lag gauge metrics, improved SIGTERM drain behavior, and a 15 percent reduction in memory footprint under steady-state load. Read the full changelog on our engineering blog.
Terraform drift is not a one-time cleanup task. It is a signal that your runbooks are incomplete. Sharing the drift-detection workflow my team runs on every prod change.

Three Go concurrency patterns I wish I had known before writing ridgeline-queue: bounded worker pools with context cancellation, backpressure via channel blocking, and graceful drain on SIGTERM. A short write-up is linked below.

Reliability tip: gate every rollout on p95 latency, not averages. Averages hide the tail that your most active users experience. We caught a 3x p95 regression last sprint that would have been invisible on a mean-only dashboard.

The Wasatch Touring Club fall trailwork weekend wrapped up yesterday. Twelve volunteers, six miles of tread rehab above Mill Creek, and one very muddy trail dog. Grateful for the folks who spend a Saturday moving rocks so the rest of us can run these trails.

The ridgeline-auth CrashLoopBackOff we hit last week was a cert mount path regression — v3.1.0 expected /etc/certs but the cert-manager volume was at /etc/ssl/rcl after the rotation. Simple but costly. Runbook updated. Hotfix ships tomorrow.

Ridgeline Cloud is attending SLC Tech Summit next month. Come talk distributed systems, reliability, and Go with our engineering team. We will have a booth and Amanda Hayes will be on the engineering leadership panel.

Debugging a CrashLoopBackOff is mostly archaeology. You are reconstructing what the process believed about the world when it died. Container logs + k8s events + previous pod logs together tell the story. Restart_count=7 is a clue, not the answer.

We added a second control plane node to the Ridgeline Cloud Kubernetes cluster last night. HA control plane in prod means a node failure no longer takes down the scheduler. Quiet and uneventful — exactly how infrastructure changes should go.

Just shipped the ridgeline-compute /metrics endpoint. Goroutine counts, worker pool utilization, and job duration histograms — all on :9091/metrics and scraped by Prometheus. Now we can actually see what the compute nodes are doing under load.

Q1 engineering retrospective at Ridgeline Cloud: cert rotation automation, Loki log retention, pgbouncer pool tuning, and consumer lag metrics all shipped. Four projects, zero P0 incidents. Proud of the team.
Engineering leaders: scope your sprints before staffing them, not after. If the work takes three engineers and you only have two, something will slip. Make that visible in planning, not in retro.

Loki 3.0 with 30-day retention in prod and 7-day in staging is live. Disk usage on node-worker-3 dropped by 40 percent overnight. Log retention policies are not optional — they are operational hygiene.

gRPC dual-serve middleware is merged into ridgeline-api main. Every endpoint can now opt into gRPC behind the grpc_migration feature flag. Next sprint: wire the actual gRPC server and test with the proto stubs.

We are growing. Four open roles on the Ridgeline Cloud engineering team: Senior Backend Engineer, Staff SRE, Platform Engineer, and Developer Advocate. Remote-friendly, competitive comp. See the careers page link below.

Hot take for distributed systems teams: your feature flag system is more important than your deployment system. You can roll back a deploy in minutes. Rolling back bad logic in a shipped binary without flags can take days.

Took the kids up to the Mirror Lake area last weekend — Ethan led the map for the first time and Lily identified three wildflowers I didn't know. Reminded me that the best way to raise Wasatch kids is just to keep showing up to the trailhead.

k6 load test gate is live in CI for ridgeline-compute. Merge to main now requires p95 < 300ms over a 60s/60-worker run against staging. First automated gate caught a 340ms regression before it hit prod. Worth every hour to set up.
